content:
# Genesis Chapter 37 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 21v–22r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_037_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_037_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 35–36

---

*The Generations of Jacob — Joseph at Seventeen (vv. 1–2)*

**37:1** And Jacob settled in the land of his father's sojournings, in the land of Canaan.

**37:2** These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was pasturing the flock with his brothers. He was a youth with the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives. And Joseph brought an evil report of them to their father.

---

*The Ketonet Passim — The Brothers' Hatred (vv. 3–4)*

**37:3** Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons, because he was the son of his old age, and he made him a ketonet passim [an ornamented robe]. **37:4** And his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, and they hated him, and they could not speak peaceably to him.

---

*Joseph's First Dream — The Sheaves (vv. 5–8)*

**37:5** And Joseph dreamed a dream and told it to his brothers, and they hated him still more.

**37:6** And he said to them, "Hear, I pray, this dream that I have dreamed: **37:7** Behold, we were binding sheaves in the midst of the field, and behold, my sheaf arose and also stood upright, and behold, your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf."

**37:8** And his brothers said to him, "Will you indeed reign over us? Will you indeed rule over us?" And they hated him still more for his dreams and for his words.

---

*Joseph's Second Dream — Sun, Moon, and Stars (vv. 9–11)*

**37:9** And he dreamed yet another dream and told it to his brothers, and he said, "Behold, I have dreamed another dream: and behold, the sun and the moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me."

**37:10** And he told it to his father and to his brothers, and his father rebuked him and said to him, "What is this dream that you have dreamed? Shall I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow ourselves down to the ground before you?"

**37:11** And his brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind.

---

*Joseph Sent to His Brothers — Shechem to Dothan (vv. 12–17)*

**37:12** And his brothers went to pasture their father's flock at Shechem. **37:13** And Israel said to Joseph, "Are not your brothers pasturing the flock at Shechem? Come, I will send you to them." And he said to him, "Here I am."

**37:14** And he said to him, "Go now, see whether it is well with your brothers and well with the flock, and bring me word." So he sent him from the Valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.

**37:15** And a man found him — and behold, he was wandering in the field. And the man asked him, "What are you seeking?" **37:16** And he said, "I am seeking my brothers. Tell me, please, where they are pasturing." **37:17** And the man said, "They have moved on from here, for I heard them say, 'Let us go to Dothan.'" So Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothan.

---

*The Brothers Plot — Reuben's Intervention (vv. 18–24)*

**37:18** And they saw him from a distance, and before he drew near to them, they plotted against him to kill him. **37:19** And they said to one another, "Behold, here comes the master of dreams. **37:20** Come now, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits, and we will say, 'A wild beast has devoured him.' And we shall see what will become of his dreams."

**37:21** And Reuben heard and delivered him from their hand, and said, "Let us not strike him mortally." **37:22** And Reuben said to them, "Shed no blood. Throw him into this pit that is in the wilderness, but lay no hand upon him" — so that he might deliver him from their hand, to restore him to his father.

**37:23** And when Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped Joseph of his robe — the ketonet passim [ornamented robe] that was on him — **37:24** and they took him and threw him into the pit. Now the pit was empty; there was no water in it.

---

*The Ishmaelite Caravan — Joseph Sold (vv. 25–28)*

**37:25** And they sat down to eat bread. And they lifted up their eyes and looked, and behold, a caravan of Ishmaelites was coming from Gilead, and their camels were carrying tragacanth, balm, and laudanum, going down to Egypt.

**37:26** And Judah said to his brothers, "What profit is it if we kill our brother and cover his blood? **37:27** Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let our hand not be upon him, for he is our brother, our own flesh." And his brothers listened to him.

**37:28** And Midianite traders passed by, and they drew up and lifted Joseph out of the pit, and they sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. And they brought Joseph to Egypt.

---

*Reuben Returns — The Pit Is Empty (vv. 29–30)*

**37:29** And Reuben returned to the pit, and behold, Joseph was not in the pit. And he tore his garments. **37:30** And he returned to his brothers and said, "The boy is gone — and I, where shall I go?"

---

*The Bloody Robe — Jacob's Grief (vv. 31–35)*

**37:31** And they took Joseph's robe and slaughtered a male goat and dipped the robe in the blood. **37:32** And they sent the ketonet passim [ornamented robe] and brought it to their father and said, "This we have found. Recognize, please — is it your son's robe or not?"

**37:33** And he recognized it and said, "My son's robe! A wild beast has devoured him. Joseph has surely been torn to pieces."

**37:34** And Jacob tore his garments and put sackcloth on his loins and mourned for his son many days. **37:35** And all his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted and said, "For I shall go down to my son to Sheol, mourning." And his father wept for him.

---

*Joseph Sold to Potiphar in Egypt (v. 36)*

**37:36** And the Medanites sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. Ketonet passim preserved as Hebrew term with bracketed gloss (vv. 3, 23, 32): New convention established**

This is the most significant translation decision in Genesis 37. The three translators diverge: EMET preserves "coat of passim" (untranslated); LOGOS renders "long ornamented robe"; PNEUMA renders "special robe." The Hebrew ketonet passim is genuinely uncertain — passim may derive from pas ("flat of the hand/foot," suggesting a garment reaching to hands and feet = long-sleeved/full-length), or from passim as "strips/pieces" (a patchwork or striped garment), or from a root meaning "ornamented" (the LXX's chiton poikilon, "many-colored tunic"). The traditional "coat of many colors" (KJV, following LXX) has been largely abandoned by modern scholarship but remains culturally iconic.

SOPHIA establishes a new convention parallel to the massebah treatment: **ketonet passim** is preserved as a Hebrew term with the bracketed gloss **[ornamented robe]**, which conveys the garment's distinctive, status-marking character without claiming certainty about its specific form. "Ornamented" is supported by the LXX tradition and by the 2 Samuel 13:18-19 parallel (where Tamar wears a ketonet passim identified as the garment of virgin princesses). This preserves the Hebrew's own specificity without over-translating.

**2. "Master of dreams" — ba'al ha-chalomot (v. 19): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render ba'al ha-chalomot hallazeh as "master of dreams" / "that master of dreams." PNEUMA renders "that dreamer." The Hebrew ba'al means "master, owner, lord" — a word of status and possession. The brothers' use of it is contemptuous: they mock Joseph as one who claims mastery over dreams, not merely as one who happens to dream. "Dreamer" captures the content but loses the sarcasm. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "the master of dreams."

**3. "Pit" — bor (vv. 22, 24, 28, 29): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render bor as "pit." PNEUMA renders "cistern." While bor can refer to a cistern (and the detail "there was no water in it" suggests a dry cistern), "pit" is the more fundamental meaning and carries the literary weight that the Joseph narrative requires — the pit foreshadows the prison (also bor, Genesis 40:15, 41:14). SOPHIA rules 2-1: "pit."

**4. Hinneni — "Here I am" (v. 13): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS preserve the classic hinneni as "Here I am." PNEUMA renders "I'm ready." The word hinneni carries enormous theological weight in Genesis — Abraham's response at Moriah (22:1, 11), Jacob's response to God (31:11, 46:2). Joseph's hinneni before his father echoes these moments and carries an undertone of readiness-for-sacrifice that "I'm ready" diminishes. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "Here I am."

**5. Medanites — v. 36: EMET's reading adopted**

The Leningrad Codex reads מְדָנִים (*Medanim*, "Medanites") at v. 36, not מִדְיָנִים (*Midyanim*, "Midianites") as in v. 28. These are two distinct gentilic forms (Medan and Midian are both sons of Abraham by Keturah, Genesis 25:2). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "Midianites" throughout, harmonizing v. 36 with v. 28. EMET correctly distinguishes the two readings in the Leningrad Codex. SOPHIA follows the primary manuscript: "Medanites" in v. 36, preserving the textual distinction that the MT deliberately maintains.

**6. "Kept the matter" — shamar et ha-davar (v. 11): All agree on sense (3-0)**

All three translators render this phrase as "kept the matter (in mind)." The verb shamar ("to keep, to guard, to watch over") here denotes mental retention — Jacob stored the dream in his memory, pondering its significance. The parallel to Luke 2:19 (Mary "kept all these things, pondering them in her heart") is noted by all three translators.

**7. Tarof toraf — infinitive absolute (v. 33): "Surely been torn to pieces" (3-0)**

All three capture the intensifying force of the infinitive absolute construction. SOPHIA renders "Joseph has surely been torn to pieces" — preserving the intensifier "surely" and the violence of the verb taraf ("to tear, to rend as a wild animal does").

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 35–36)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 37:3 | כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים (ketonet passim) | χιτῶνα ποικίλον ("many-colored tunic") | Preserve Hebrew term: ketonet passim [ornamented robe] |
| 37:28 | בְּעֶשְׂרִים כֶּסֶף ("twenty of silver") | εἴκοσι χρυσῶν ("twenty gold pieces") | Follow MT: "twenty pieces of silver" |
| 37:35 | שְׁאֹלָה (Sheol) | εἰς ᾅδου (Hades) | Follow MT: "Sheol" — not Hades |
| 37:36 | מְדָנִים (Medanites) | Μαδιηναῖοι (Madianaioi / Midianites) | Follow MT: "Medanites" — distinct from v. 28 "Midianites" |
| 37:36 | פּוֹטִיפַר (Potiphar) | Πετεφρης (Petephres) | Follow MT: "Potiphar" |
| 37:36 | שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים (captain of the guard) | ἀρχιμάγειρος (chief cook) | Follow MT: "captain of the guard" |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
